draft.introduction: Desiderius Erasmus's Praise of Folly (1511) is the masterpiece of Christian humanism. Written as a speech by the goddess Folly herself, it satirizes every level of society and ends with a startling argument that Christian faith is itself a form of divine madness.

translation.text: However mortal folk may commonly speak of me—for I am not unaware how ill Folly is spoken of, even among the most foolish—still it is I, and I alone, who have the power to gladden both gods and men. This much is proved simply by my appearance here before this great assembly: the moment I began to speak, every face immediately brightened with a fresh and unusual cheerfulness. You have smoothed your brows, and you have applauded me with a frank and hearty laugh. Every one of you whom I see before me seems drunk with nectar, like the gods of Homer. A moment ago you were sitting gloomy and tense, as if you had come back from visiting Trophonius' cave.

translator_notes.note: Erasmus wrote the Praise of Folly in a week while staying at the home of Thomas More (to whom the work's Latin title, Moriae Encomium, is a pun on More's name). The work's subversive brilliance lies in its layered irony: Folly praises herself, but her critique of human vanity is often devastatingly accurate, leaving the reader unsure whether to laugh at Folly or agree with her.
